title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Reading the Sky: Ancient Astronomers and Their Legacy
content.text: Long before telescopes, satellites or computer simulations, ancient peoples across the globe turned their eyes to the night sky and began asking questions that still drive science today. From the stone circles of Britain to the stepped pyramids of Mesoamerica, early astronomers built monuments of extraordinary precision to track the movements of the sun, moon and stars. (37) [37] Their observations, accumulated over generations, formed the foundation on which modern astronomy would eventually be built. [/37] || The Babylonians, working in present-day Iraq around three thousand years ago, developed the first systematic records of planetary motion. They tracked the movements of Venus, Mars, Jupiter and Saturn with remarkable accuracy, producing tables that allowed them to predict eclipses. (38) [38] These cuneiform tablets represent the earliest known attempt to apply mathematical reasoning to astronomical phenomena. [/38] || In ancient Egypt, the alignment of temples and pyramids with specific stars was not merely symbolic. The rising of Sirius, for example, coincided with the annual flooding of the Nile and signalled the start of the agricultural year. (39) [39] Astronomy was therefore inseparable from agriculture, religion and the organisation of civic life. [/39] || Greek astronomers built on these earlier traditions to develop theoretical models of the cosmos. Eratosthenes calculated the Earth's circumference with striking accuracy in the third century BC, using nothing more than the angle of shadows at noon in two different cities. (40) [40] Such achievements remind us that ancient science was capable of remarkable intellectual leaps even without modern instrumentation. [/40] || The Arab scholars of the Islamic Golden Age preserved and extended Greek astronomical knowledge at a time when much of Europe had lost access to it. (41) [41] Many of the star names still used today — Aldebaran, Betelgeuse, Deneb — are Arabic in origin. [/41] || Modern astronomy continues to honour this long lineage. (42) [42] Every time a researcher plots a planet's orbit or names a newly discovered star, they are participating in a tradition of sky-watching that stretches back thousands of years. [/42]

paragraphs.A: Their written records, inscribed on clay tablets, survived to be studied by later civilisations.
paragraphs.B: This shows that precise sky-watching had profound practical consequences for ancient societies.
paragraphs.C: Without their translations and commentaries, centuries of astronomical progress might have been lost.
paragraphs.D: Aristarchus went further still, proposing that the Earth orbited the Sun — an idea dismissed at the time but proved correct two millennia later.
paragraphs.E: The discipline has always been cumulative, each era adding to the work of those who came before.
paragraphs.F: They were, in effect, the first scientists — observers who sought patterns and drew conclusions from them.
paragraphs.G: Their instruments were simple by today's standards, but their methods were genuinely scientific.

questions.explanation: F describes ancient astronomers as the first scientists who sought patterns, connecting with the introduction about their scientific questions.
A mentions that their written records on clay tablets survived to be studied later, referring to the Babylonian cuneiform tablets.
B asserts that observing the sky had profound practical consequences, supporting the connection between astronomy, agriculture and civic life in Egypt.
D adds another example of Greek intellectual achievement (Aristarchus and heliocentrism), reinforcing the statement about the great intellectual leaps of ancient science.
C notes that without Arabic translations the astronomical progress would have been lost, explaining the role of Arab scholars before mentioning the names of stars.
E describes astronomy as a cumulative discipline, closing the article with the idea of historical continuity.
